danger_signals.visual_form: Bright contrasting bullets, typically red or yellow against dark space backgrounds
Large mechanical creature designs filling screen space, distinctive silhouettes
Visual boundaries or environmental obstacles at stage edges
Dense clustering of smaller enemy sprites in formation

safety_signals.visual_form: Floating collectible sprites with distinct color and icon designs
safety_signals.meaning: Weapon enhancement or temporary protection available

safety_signals.visual_form: Visible gaps in enemy formation or projectile patterns
Background parallax scrolling, environmental transition indicators

faction_indicators.color_identity: Typically white or bright primary color for player ship
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Small ship sprite with distinctive design
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Player projectiles use distinct visual signature (usually brighter/faster than enemy fire)

faction_indicators.color_identity: Dark metallic tones, reds, or contrasting enemy faction colors
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Mechanical/organic hybrid creature designs specific to Darius II's aquatic-mechanical aesthetic
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Various enemy ship types distinguished by hull design, color markings, movement patterns
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Enemy projectile trails distinct from player fire; formation patterns indicate unit type/threat level

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Background color shifts, more complex parallax layering, denser sprite density on screen
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy spawn rates increase; formation patterns become more complex; boss encounters feature more varied attack phases

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Player projectile effects become more elaborate; screen effects intensify when weapons fire
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can clear enemy formations faster; screen control improves

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Large singular entity dominates screen center; background may shift to thematic arena; visual effects heighten
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Boss exhibits telegraphed attack patterns; multiple distinct attack phases; requires sustained player engagement

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Screen transition effects; visual theme shift indicating new environment/world zone
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy types change; background aesthetic transforms; difficulty recalibrates

environmental_storytelling.example: Each stage features distinct background art and enemy designs that suggest different locations in game world (mechanical fortresses, organic vessels, etc.), communicating narrative progression without text
Enemy ship types and formations suggest faction military hierarchy and technological capabilities; larger/more ornate designs indicate command vessels or higher-threat units
Stage backgrounds use color shifts (blues for aquatic zones, reds for hostile territories, greens for organic/living spaces) to communicate location and atmosphere
Multiple background layers move at different speeds to suggest spatial depth and establish whether player is engaging surface-level threats or deeper combat zones

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Move in predictable geometric formations; projectile fire in rhythmic patterns; circular or linear approach vectors
Acceleration toward player; projectile fire intensifies; formation breaks to pursue
Deliberate movement pattern; projectile spreads in identifiable formations; telegraphed maneuvers
Attack patterns become more chaotic; projectile density increases; movement speed may accelerate; distinct visual effects signal phase transition
Single-stream weapon fire; limited maneuverability; projectile impact visible on screen
Multi-stream or spread weapon fire; enhanced visual effects on projectiles; screen fills with player firepower signature

notes: Darius II (Genesis, 1990) is a horizontal-scrolling shooter that communicates world-state primarily through visual and behavioral manifestation rather than explicit UI. Faction distinction is minimal as this is a single-player combat scenario; however, the game clearly differentiates player forces from enemy forces through color, projectile appearance, and entity design. The game uses classic shmup grammar: color-coded threats, formation-based enemy AI, and escalating visual complexity as difficulty increases. Environmental storytelling occurs through stage-specific theming and enemy design diversity. No narrative text elements complicate the visual language—the game relies entirely on visual signals for threat assessment and world-state communication. Power-up mechanics create clear visual feedback loops (brighter/more powerful weapon effects = safer player state). Boss encounters serve as difficulty milestones marked by dramatic visual presentation. The Achaemenid historical source provided (Darius II Wikipedia article) appears to be a retrieval error and does not contain game-specific information.
